name: Longstock
overview: Longstock is a thatched-cottage village of around 500 residents on the west bank of the upper River Test, about two miles north of Stockbridge in the Test Valley district. Almost every house on the village street is timber-framed and thatched, with the prominent exception of the Peat Spade Inn — a Victorian gastropub-with-rooms that is now the village's main commercial anchor. The parish church of St Mary's was largely rebuilt in the 1880s, but the village's older fabric is exceptional: Longstock is one of the most visually consistent surviving 17th- and 18th-century chalk-stream villages in southern England, which is partly why John Spedan Lewis bought the Longstock Park estate and made his family home there from 1945. Longstock Park (and the neighbouring Leckford Estate) remains owned by the John Lewis Partnership, with Longstock House used by Partnership senior management and Longstock Park Water Gardens open to the public on selected Sundays April–September.
character: Pure thatched chalk-stream village: timber-frame, lime-render, thatch-and-deep-eaves cottages strung along Longstock Road and Cottage Lane. Tight conservation area; planning constraints are exceptionally strict. Building stock predominantly 17th–18th century with Victorian estate cottages.
amenities: The Peat Spade Inn (gastropub with rooms)
Direct riverside access to upper Test beats
Within South Wessex Downs Special Landscape Area

schools.name: Test Valley School / Danebury School, Stockbridge

transport.roads: A3057 (through village); A30 ~2 mi; A303 ~5 mi; M3 J8 ~14 mi

thingsToDo: Longstock Park Water Gardens (Partnership-owned, public Sundays)
Trout/grayling fishing on the upper Test
pros: Outstanding visual character — one of England's best surviving thatched chalk-stream villages
Direct access to upper Test fishing beats
Andover station 5 mi for direct Waterloo trains
Adjacent to Leckford Estate amenities (shop, café, water gardens)
cons: Tight conservation regime restricts alterations and extensions
Single pub; no shop or post office in the village itself
Heritage-grade prices for thatched stock with high maintenance
Secondary catchment school rated Inadequate in 2022
whoItSuits: Buyers willing to pay a heritage premium for a postcard thatched cottage in one of England's most photographed chalk-stream villages, and to accept the maintenance cycle that comes with thatch (re-thatching roughly every 25–35 years). Particularly suited to anglers, second-homers, downsizers and cash buyers. The village's status as 'mostly Partnership-owned' beyond its private street means open-market stock comes up less frequently than the village size suggests. Poor fit for first-time buyers and anyone needing a daily train commute without a car.
primarySupermarket: Waitrose, Stockbridge (2.5 mi); Sainsbury's, Andover (5.5 mi)
